coredata.dc:title: Yellow emission carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive OFF-ON sensing of ferric and pyrophosphate ions in living cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Jinyan
Yang, Ying
Shao, Taili
Qi, Shuangqing
Zhang, Ping
Zhuo, Shujuan
Zhu, Changqing
coredata.dc:description: A simple “OFF-ON” fluorescent system was proposed for selective and sensitive detection of ferric ion (Fe3+) and pyrophosphate (PPi) in living cells. The method was constructed based on the bright yellow emission of carbon dots (y-CDs), which were prepared using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the precursor via a facile hydrothermal treatment. The as-obtained y-CDs, with an average size of 2.6 nm, exhibited an excitation-independent emission peak at 574 nm. The fluorescence of y-CDs can be remarkably quenched by Fe3+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. Interestingly, the quenched fluorescence can be recovered regularly upon addition of PPi, showing a promising detection for PPi. The linear ranges for Fe3+ and PPi detections were 0.05–80 and 0.5–120 μM, respectively, and the corresponding limit of detections (LODs) were 22.1 and 73.9 nM. As we proved the y-CDs have negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, further application to the fluorescence imaging of intracellular Fe3+ and PPi were conducted, suggesting the prepared y-CDs can be used to monitor Fe3+ and PPi variation in living cells. Overall, our developed y-CDs-based OFF-ON switch fluorescent probe has the advantages of simplicity, agility, high sensitivity and selectivity, which provides a promising platform for environmental and biology applications, and paves a new avenue for monitoring the hydrolysis process of adenosine triphosphate disodium salt (ATP) by detection of PPi in organisms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pyrophosphate
Carbon dots
Fe3+ ions
Cell imaging
OFF-ON sensing
Fluorescence quenching
Fluorescence recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and PPi in aqueous buffer
Detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution
An “OFF-ON” signal for detection of PPi
Cell imaging for Fe 3+ and PPi biomonitoring

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM image (inset) of the y-CDs. Scale bars, 50 nm and 2 nm (inset), respectively. (B) Particle size distribution of the y-CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of y-CDs (black line), excitation (blue line) and emission spectrum (red line). Insets are the photographic images of y-CDs under daylight (left) and UV light (right) at 365 nm excitation. (D) Emission spectra of the as-prepared y-CDs at the different excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) The FTIR spectrum of the as-prepared y-CDs, (B) The XRD pattern of the as-prepared y-CDs, (C) Full XPS spectra of the y-CDs, (D, E, F) high-resolution C1s, N1s, O1s XPS spectra of the y-CDs.

(A) Effects of different metal ions on the ratio of fluorescence intensity of the y-CDs. The concentration of analytes is 1 mM, and the number of repeat experiments is 3 times). (B) Fluorescence spectra of the y-CDs (15 μg/mL) in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ from 0.05 to 80 μM. (C) Linear relationship between the fluorescence ratio and the concentration of Fe3+ (F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the free y-CDs and F is the fluorescence intensity of y-CDs with the addition of Fe3+, n = 5). (D) Photographs of the y-CDs with addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ under irradiation by a 365 nm-excited UV lamp.

(A) Flourescence recovery of the ensemble of y-CDs and Fe3+ (15 μg/mL and 90 μM, respectively) in the presence of different concentrations of PPi. (B) Relationship between fluorescence restoration of the y-CDs versus concentration of PPi, where F0 is fluorescence intensity of the y-CDs with addition of Fe3+ and F is the fluorescence intensity of ensemble of y-CDs and Fe3+ with addition of PPi. (The number of repeat experiments is 5 times). (C) Selectivity of the ensemble of y-CDs and Fe3+ (15 μg/mL and 90 μM, respectively) toward PPi (100 μM). (The concentration of analytes is 1 mM. The number of repeat experiments is 5 times). (D) Corresponding photographs of the ensemble of y-CDs and Fe3+ with increasing concentrations of PPi under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation.

Confocal microscopic (A) bright-field. (B) dark-field images of A-549 cells incubated with y-CDs (0.8 mg/mL) for 4 h. (C. D. E. F) Confocal microscopic images of treated A-549 cells with different concentrations of Fe3+ (0.39, 1.10, 1.82 and 3.05 mM), respectively.

Confocal microscopic (A) bright-field, (B) dark-field images of A-549 cells co-incubated with y-CDs (0.8 mg/mL) and Fe3+ (1.82 mg/mL) for 4 h. (C. D. E. F) Confocal microscopic images of co-stained A-549 cells using the y-CDs and Fe3+ followed by treating with different concentrations of PPi (0.35, 1.01, 1.64 and 2.20 mM), respectively.
Schematic illustration of the competitive interaction mechanism between CDs - Fe3+ and Fe3+ - PPi.

head.ce:title: Yellow emission carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive OFF-ON sensing of ferric and pyrophosphate ions in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Key Laboratory of Chemo-Biosensing, Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu, 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Key Laboratory of Chemo-Biosensing, Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anhui Key Laboratory of Chemo-Biosensing, Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu, 241000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Provincial Engineering Research Center for Polysaccharide Drugs, School of Pharmacy, Wannan Medical College, Wuhu 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Provincial Engineering Research Center for Polysaccharide Drugs

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anhui Provincial Engineering Research Center for Polysaccharide Drugs, School of Pharmacy, Wannan Medical College, Wuhu 241000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using competitive coordination effects between two pairs of reactions, Fe3+ and PPi can be detected by the as-synthesized y-CDs with bright yellow emission fluorescence. Moreover, the y-CDs can be further utilized in intracellular Fe3+ and PPi imaging and biomonitoring.

A simple “OFF-ON” fluorescent system was proposed for selective and sensitive detection of ferric ion (Fe3+) and pyrophosphate (PPi) in living cells. The method was constructed based on the bright yellow emission of carbon dots (y-CDs), which were prepared using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the precursor via a facile hydrothermal treatment. The as-obtained y-CDs, with an average size of 2.6 nm, exhibited an excitation-independent emission peak at 574 nm. The fluorescence of y-CDs can be remarkably quenched by Fe3+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. Interestingly, the quenched fluorescence can be recovered regularly upon addition of PPi, showing a promising detection for PPi. The linear ranges for Fe3+ and PPi detections were 0.05–80 and 0.5–120 μM, respectively, and the corresponding limit of detections (LODs) were 22.1 and 73.9 nM. As we proved the y-CDs have negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, further application to the fluorescence imaging of intracellular Fe3+ and PPi were conducted, suggesting the prepared y-CDs can be used to monitor Fe3+ and PPi variation in living cells. Overall, our developed y-CDs-based OFF-ON switch fluorescent probe has the advantages of simplicity, agility, high sensitivity and selectivity, which provides a promising platform for environmental and biology applications, and paves a new avenue for monitoring the hydrolysis process of adenosine triphosphate disodium salt (ATP) by detection of PPi in organisms.

ce:section.ce:para: Fe(III) ions is the key component in human bodies or living tissues due to its important role in biological systems [1–3]. In the body cells of living organisms, excessive Fe3+ ions is toxic which may cause vomiting, diarrhea and intestinal damage. Moreover, Fe3+ is highly oxidized, while substances such as vitamins are reductive. Therefore, it is of vital importance to design a simple and sensitive Fe3+ probe. In recent years, fluorescent sensing, which has been screened as one of the most economic and efficient ways to detect metal ions, is free from sophisticated instrument and complicated procedures [4].

Pyrophosphate (PPi), a dimer form of inorganic phosphate (Pi), is an important biological anion and plays a crucial role in cell metabolism, starch transformation regulation [5], adenosine triphosphate disodium salt (ATP) hydrolysis, DNA and RNA polymerization [6] and other basic physiological processes. The concentrations of PPi can indicate the key information such as DNA replication. They also can be served as indicators in cancer diagnosis by monitoring the telomerase prolongation process [7]. Obviously, a sensitive and efficient PPi probe, which can not only be used for PPi detection in aqueous solution, but also can monitor the PPi content in cells, is urgently needed.

Recently, PPi sensing has received an increasing amount of attention, and many PPi fluorescent probes have also been developed [8–17]. Due to unique electronic, optical and magnetic properties, nanomaterials have been wildly explored and developed as chemical sensors for PPi sensing [18]. Particularly, various transition metals (Mz+: Fe, Cu, Zn, Eu, and Tb, etc.), can be served as bridges for detecting PPi. For examples, You et al. [19,20] have reported a novel triangular gold nanoprism as a turn-on probe for a highly selective and sensitive detection of copper ions and PPi. Zhao et al. have developed europium nanocomposites for rapid and selective detection of pyrophosphate [21]. Mawai et al. [22] have constructed a compartmental chemosensor probe for the selective recognition of zinc and pyrophosphate ions over other transition metal ions via a fluorescence “ON” strategy. The nanomaterials-based sensors are simple, sensitive, which open up a new avenue for PPi sensing. However, these PPi detection strategies as well as their sensor synthesis are often complicated. Moreover, most of the PPi-specific fluorescence sensors were reported to be applied in aqueous solution, but few of them were utilized in intracellular sensing.

Due to excellent solubility, easy synthesis and functionalization, chemical inertness, low toxicity, and resistance of photobleaching, carbon dots (CDs) with strong fluorescence have generated enormous excitement in the fields of biological imaging and biomedicine over the past two decades [23]. In view of the dimensionality, the quantum confinement results in their amazing optical features, and the specific spectral bands are very suitable for sensing [24,25]. Moreover, the synthetic techniques of fluorescent CDs are mature, thereby providing great conveniences for sensing a wide range of analytes [26–34], including the detection of PPi. The sensing mechanism was believed as the competitive coordination effects between MZ+ - CDs interaction and MZ+ - PPi interaction. Although some reported fluorescent CDs have an excellent performance for sensing PPi, the majority of them have intense emission only in the blue or green wavelength regions, while the emission radiation in the region from yellow to red is usually very weak [35,36]. This limitation hinders the further application of CDs, particularly in the biology-relevant fields, as the autofluorescence of biological matrices is usually blue while biological tissues are prone to be damaged by ultraviolet excitation light [37]. Recently, some research groups have devoted to integrating the long-wavelength CDs into the biological fields [38–42] and other applications [43]. However, to our knowledge, there are few reports on PPi studies in cells so far. Due to the direct selective and sensitive sensing of PPi in cancer research [44] and the diagnosis, therapy of arthritic diseases [45], it is necessary to develop fluorescent CDs that can be sensitive to PPi not only in aqueous solution but also in cells with strong long-wavelength fluorescence emission characteristics.

Herein, the carbon dots with a bright yellow emission (y-CDs) were prepared using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as a carbon source by a modified hydrothermal treatment on the basis of a previously reported method [46]. Then, an OFF-ON fluorescent sensing nano-platform, i.e., OFF signal (fluorescence quenching) due to the interaction between Fe3+ and the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the y-CDs, ON signal (fluorescence recovery) due to the dominated competitive interaction between Fe3+ and PPi, was realized for the assay of Fe3+ and PPi based on the characteristic of bright yellow fluorescence of y-CDs (Scheme 1 ). As we proved that the y-CDs probe shows negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, further utilization in intracellular Fe3+ and PPi imaging and biomonitoring was also fully conducted.

All the Chemical agents used were of analytic grade. OPD and 1000 Da cutoff dialysis bags were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, P. R. China). HEPES buffer solution was purchased from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. Phosphoric acid (H3PO4), Trypsin cell digestive juice, Dulbecco’s phosphate buffered saline (PBS), Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM, Lonza), Foetal Bovine Serum (FBS, Lonza), 1% penicillin/streptomycin, and the antibiotic agents were obtained from Hefei Ruijie Biotechnology Co. Ltd. A-549 cells were purchased from Sangon (Shanghai, China). All of the Chemical reagents were used without any treatment. Glassware was washed with aqua regia (conc. HCl: conc. HNO3, volume ratio = 3:1), and then rinsed with ultrapure water (18.25 MU cm, Mini-Q).

Fluorescence data were collected using an FS5 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were carried out on an HT-7700 (Tokyo, Japan) transmission electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB 250XI electron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum date was recorded using a PerkinElmer FTIR spectrophotometer (PE-983, USA) using KBr pellet at room temperature. X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD) was carried out on a D8 Adance (brock company, Germany). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured by a U-2910 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescent photographs of solution under UV lamp of 365 nm were operated with a ZF-1 ultraviolet analyzer from Shanghai City Jihui Scientific Analyze Instrument Co., Ltd. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed on an FLS920 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was measured at 570 nm with a microplate reader (HIGHLAND PARK 05404-0998 USA). Confocal microscopy images were taken with a LeicaSP8 confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica company, Germany).

The y-CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. In brief, 0.2160 g OPD was added into 30 mL water under bath sonication until it was completely dissolved to homogeneous solution. Then the above solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. After the obtained yellow solution was cooled down to room temperature naturally, the solution was collected by removing larger particles through centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, the aqueous solution was purified using a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000 Da) for 24 h to remove the impurities. After purification, we obtained the CDs with yellow light emission fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and PPi in aqueous buffer
ce:section.ce:para: All reactions and measurements below were performed at room temperature. The fluorescence spectrum with excitation wavelength was set at 410 nm with photomultiplier tube voltage 900 V. The slit widths of the excitation and emission were both fixed at 10 nm. A fluorescence assay for Fe3+ and PPi proceeded under the following procedures. Briefly, 200 μL of the y-CDs (0.15 mg/mL) solutions was put into 800 μL of HEPES (10 mM, pH 6.0), followed by the addition of different amounts of Fe3+ ions. Then the mixture was diluted to 2 mL with ultrapure water. In the selective assay of Fe3+, Fe3+ was replaced by K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, and Ba2+ (1 mM) and the fluorescence spectra were measured under the same conditions.

The vitro cytotoxicity of y-CDs was assessed by the standard MTT assay. The A-549 lung cancer cells were cultured for 24 h at 37 °C in a 96-well plate with a density of 5000 cells/mL. Then the y-CDs of different concentrations (0, 0.075, 0.15, 0.3, 0.6, 1.2 and 2.4 mg/mL) were added into each well and incubated for an additional 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2. Subsequently, 10 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL) was added into each well. After incubating for another 4 h at 37 °C, the media were removed, and 100 μL DMSO was added to each well and shaken for 2 min. After another 3 h of culture, absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader (HIGHLAND PARK 05404–0998 USA).

According to the above cytotoxicity test, the y-CDs were basically non-toxic and were used in biological imaging (see below). A-549 cancer cells were cultured in DMEM with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin streptomycin with 5% CO2 as media at 37 °C. The y-CDs of 0.8 mg/mL were added into the cells for incubation of 4 h at 37 °C, then the cells were washed thoroughly with pH 7.4 PBS buffer to remove the cell culture medium. Finally, the cell images were acquired with a LeicaSP8 confocal microscope equipped with a laser of 405 nm, and emissions were caught with the yellow (577–597 nm) single band fluorescence filters.

The y-CDs-stained A-549 cells were treated with different concentrations of Fe3+ in 500 μL DMEM media and 1 mL HEPES (pH 6.0) for 3 min. The co-staining A-549 cells by the y-CDs and Fe3+ (1.82 mM) were treated with different concentrations PPi for 3 min. Fluorescence images of the cells were collected with a LeicaSP8 confocal microscope. Yellow emissions were recorded using a 577–597 nm band-pass filter upon 405 nm excitation.

The y-CDs were easily prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method using OPD as carbon and nitrogen sources [47]. The as-obtained y-CDs have a good dispersion in water. The morphology of the y-CDs was characterized by TEM. As can be observed in Fig. 1 A and 1B, the synthesized y-CDs have a spherical, uniform structure with an average size of 2.6 nm. The clear lattice fringes with a space of 0.25 nm are observed in the HRTEM image (Fig. 1A, inset), which is corresponding to the (1 0 0) in-plane lattice of graphite, indicative of a good crystallinity of the y-CDs [23,48].

Subsequently, the optical properties of the y-CDs were examined. The UV–vis absorption of the y-CDs was shown in Fig. 1C. It clearly shows that the as-prepared y-CDs have an absorption band at 286 nm, which can be attributed to the π–π* electron transition [38], and the broader visible absorption peak belongs to the complex surface states [49]. Excitation spectra of the y-CDs show that the maximum excitation is at 410 nm (Fig. 1C), which is consistent with the UV–vis spectrum. The fluorescence emission is at 574 nm when the optimal excitation (410 nm) is given. As illustrated in the inset of Fig. 1C, the obtained y-CDs show bright yellow light under the UV light (365 nm).

The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1D) show the fluorescence emission peak of y-CDs at 574 nm is excitation-independent with the excitation wavelength changing from 330 to 450 nm. This scenario may be due to the relative uniform surface states and size distribution [50–53]. In addition, the y-CDs exhibit bright yellow luminescence under excitation at 410 nm. In Fig. S1, the fluorescence decay of y-CDs monitored at 574 nm is in line with biexponential fitting curve with an average lifetime of 1.98 ns. Furthermore, an absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the y-CDs was determined by a widely used method [54] in which the calculated QY of the y-CDs in aqueous solution is about 8.51% under 410 nm excitation.

In order to investigate the optical characteristics of the y-CDs, the effect of pH and time on the stabilities were evaluated. As shown in Fig. S2A, the fluorescence has a certain quenching phenomenon under different pH values [55,56]. At acidic condition (pH < 4), the luminescence is quenched, but the y-CDs is extremely stable in neutral and alkaline environments. Under the ultraviolet lamp for 90 min, the peak of emission intensity is basically unchanged (Fig. S2B). So it can be deduced that the y-CDs are stable enough.

The functional groups on the surface of the y-CDs were studied by FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the absorption bands at 3200–3500 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of - OH and NH. The peaks at 2930 cm−1 and 2853 cm−1 reveal the presence of the - CH2 groups and C C bond. The - CN band is observed at 1382 cm−1, while the peak at 1096 cm−1 indicates the presence of the COC. The CO vibration shifts to 1635 cm−1. The above results demonstrate that the surfaces of y-CDs are coated with hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl and carboxylic groups), accounting for the excellent aqueous solubility and hydrophilicity. Moreover, the yellow emission was proved to be closely associated with the surface O-contained species [41]. The XRD pattern (Fig. 2B), which displays a broad diffraction peak centered at about 25° (0.34 nm), indicating that the y-CDs are composed of small crystalline cores with a disordered surface [57].

To further determine the elemental composition and valence states of the y-CDs, XPS measurements were carried out. The XPS data confirms that the functional groups are from C, N and O elements of the y-CDs. Full XPS (Fig. 2C) of the y-CDs clearly shows that three typical peaks at 286.4, 400.1, and 535.8 eV are attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. High-resolution XPS analysis of the C 1s band can be divided into three different peaks (Fig. 2D), corresponding to CC/CC (284.3 eV), C O/C N (285.8 eV) and CO (288.0 eV), respectively. This situation indicates that the hydrophilic groups are widely presented on the surfaces of y-CDs, in agreement with the corresponding FTIR spectrum. The N 1s peaks at 399.1 and 400.7 eV (Fig. 2E) suggest that most of the nitrogen elements are in the form of graphite N and amino N [46]. Moreover, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 531.5 and 532.3 eV, which corresponds to CO, and COC, respectively (Fig. 2F). The surface composition of y-CDs determined by XPS spectra is exactly consistent with the FTIR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: The corresponding optical responses of our constructed sensing system at different stages are shown in Fig. S3. In the presence of Fe3+, the as-prepared y-CDs with bright fluorescence (black curve, Fig. S3A) are efficiently quenched (red curve). This phenomenon can be clearly observed by the naked eyes under the UV lamp as shown in Fig. S3B, which is ascribed to the interaction between Fe3+ and y-CDs. Thus, the y-CDs can be utilized as the probe to detection of Fe3+. In order to obtain the optimal conditions for Fe3+ sensing, pH value, carbonization temperature and incubation time of y-CDs were optimized. As shown in Fig. S4A and S4B, the optimal pH value and carbonization temperature are found to be 6.0 and 160 °C, respectively. And for the incubation time, the response between y-CDs and Fe3+ ions is fast and the stability is quickly reached (Fig. S4C). To appraise the selectivity of the probe towards Fe3+, the response behavior of the probe to different metal ions were measured. The spectrophotometric changes of the probe upon addition of metal ions (1 µM) such as K+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+ are shown in Fig. 3 A. Although the concentration of other metal ions is 10 times higher than Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of y-CDs decreases remarkably in the present of Fe3+, along with the negligible changes for the other metallic cations. These results suggest that the probe has a high selectivity towards Fe3+ ions. Fig. 3B shows the fluorescence intensity of y-CDs at 574 nm decreases considerably with the increase in concentrations of Fe3+ from 0.05 to 80 μM. It can be attributed to the strong coordination capability between Fe3+ and the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the y-CDs [37,50,51]. As provided in Fig. 3C, a good linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity changes of y-CDs (log (F/F0)) and the concentration of Fe3+ ions is exhibited (R2 = 0.998). According to the simple linear equation y = 0.011 x + 0.006, the limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 22.1 nM. Interestingly, the color change of all reaction systems at different Fe3+ concentrations can be observed vividly by the naked eye under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 3D). The change of photoluminescence is highly favorable for the practical applications in water solution and biological probing field.

Then, the quenching mechanism of y-CDs induced by Fe3+ was investigated. The measurements of decay lifetimes would be the most effective way to differentiate dynamic (lifetime changed) and static quenching (lifetime almost unchaged) [58]. The fluorescence decay patterns of the y-CDs in the absence or presence of Fe3+ ions are recorded in Fig. S5. It can be observed that the fluorescence lifetime of y-CDs is 1.98 ns and the change of fluorescence lifetime after adding Fe3+ ions is almost negligible, thus the static quenching may play a major role in such a switch-off process. Generally, static quenching occurs when a nonfluorescent ground-state complex is formed through the strong interaction between the quencher and fluorophore [59]. Moreover, the change of ground state will cause the perturbation of the absorption spectrum of the fluorophore. To further confirm the mechanism, we investigated the absorption spectrum of y-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions. As shown in Fig. S6 A, in the presence of Fe3+, the band shows a 30 nm red-shift and the intensity increases sharply on the absorption spectrum of the as-prepared probe, indicative of strong interaction between y-CDs and Fe3+, and the formation of ground state complex. These results also verify that the static quenching is responsible for the phenomena of fluorescence quenching of the y-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: An “OFF-ON” signal for detection of PPi
ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantages of the good selectivity of the y-CDs towards Fe3+ and the strong complexing ability of PPi with Fe3+, we then carried out an assay for PPi-based y-CDs/Fe3+ system. As can be seen from Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture recovers rapidly and even exceeded the original 100%, and the incubation time is only 30 s after PPi is added, indicating that it is a very rapid detection method for PPi. Also, the response exhibits a good dependence in the range of 0.5–120 μM for PPi. In order to lower the LOD of PPi sensing, the optimal concentration of Fe3+ (90 μM) is chosen in the following research and the mixture is kept incubation for 5 min at room temperature (Fig. S4D). The linear dependent relationship between log(F/F0) and the concentrations of PPi from 0.5 to 120 μM is shown in Fig. 4B. The linear equation is y = 0.005 × - 0.003 and the LOD for PPi is calculated to be 73.9 nM.

To evaluate the selectivity of the y-CDs probe toward PPi, subsequently, the fluorescence responses of different anions to Fe3+ ions were recorded. As shown in Fig. 4C, even the concentration of these anions is 10 times with respect to PPi, only the fluorescence recovery response of PPi is significant while the rest are negligible, indicative of a pronounced fluorescence “ON” characteristic. These results indicate that the fluorescence OFF-ON strategy of y-CDs can be used for the detection of PPi with a high selectivity. It is worth mentioning that as shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence brightness of y-CDs in the present of Fe3+ ions gradually increases under UV lamp (λex = 365 nm) irradiation with the increase in PPi concentration. The results show that the Fe3+ ions have a chelated ability to PPi [60]. When the PPi is added, the absorption peak caused by the addition of Fe3+ ions disappears (Fig. S6A), indicating that the binding interaction between Fe3+ and PPi is stronger than that of between Fe3+ and oxygen-containing species in y-CDs [61]. Therefore, fluorescence intensity of the y-CDs is enhanced again after adding PPi. As shown in Fig. S6B, the fluorescence intensity of Fe3+-y-CDs with the addition of PPi increases sharply and maintains stable in 90 min. In view of their good selectivity and sensitivity to Fe3+ ions and PPi, the y-CDs are expected to serve as an OFF-ON signal sequential sensing reagents. In addition, on comparison with the results in the literatures (Table S1), our method has a broader linear range and a lower LOD. More importantly, the y-CDs with a favorite advantage, i.e., negligible damage to cells, provide an ideal platform for realizing intracellular Fe3+ and PPi monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging for Fe 3+ and PPi biomonitoring
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the good detection results and high selectivity of the y-CDs towards Fe3+ ions and PPi, we further applied the y-CDs for Fe3+ ions and PPi sensing in living cells. The cytotoxicity of the y-CDs was evaluated using MTT assay. A-549 cells were incubated for 24 h with the y-CDs. The cell survival rate is around 90% even at a y-CDs concentration of 2.4 mg/mL (in Fig. S8). This concentration is 3 times higher than the concentration used in cell imaging (0.8 mg/mL), indicative of the low cytotoxicity of the prepared y-CDs. Moreover, the y-CDs are stabilized in the concentration of NaCl between 0 and 100 mM (Fig. S7), demonstrating a good cell compatibility of the y-CDs.

Then, cell imaging experiments were carried out. In Fig. 5 A, it can be clearly observed that A-549 cells are in good morphologies. The laser scanning confocal microscopy images show the presence of an obvious yellow fluorescence, suggesting that our prepared y-CDs successfully enter into the A-549 cells by endocytosis, where they are mostly located in the cytoplasm.

Next, the prepared y-CDs were used for Fe3+ imaging in cells. As shown in Fig. 5, the fluorescence of y-CDs decreases significantly with adding different concentrations of Fe3+ ions into the cells. When the concentration of Fe3+ is 0.39 mM, there is a significant decrease in the intracellular fluorescence (Fig. 5C), which may be due to the fact that a small amount of Fe3+ enter into the cells. As the Fe3+ concentration gradually increases, the intracellular fluorescence becomes significantly darker at 1.10–3.05 mM, indicating that the quenching effect of the y-CDs probe is highlighted (Fig. 5D and 5F). Therefore, resembling to the situation of the fluorescence phenomenon in aqueous solution, the probe can be used to monitor Fe3+ ions in the cells. However, the sensitivity and speed of sensing is lower than those in aqueous solution, which may be due to the high adsorption capacity of cell membrane to Fe3+ ions [62].

Then, we added PPi to the cells incubated with the y-CDs and Fe3+ ions. As shown in Fig. 6 , the intracellular fluorescence increases significantly as the PPi concentration increasing. When PPi concentration is 0.35 mM, the intracellular fluorescence brightness increases slightly (Fig. 6C), indicating that PPi also enters into the cells. When the concentration is in the range of 1.02–2.20 mM, the fluorescence intensity of the y-CDs increases significantly (Fig. 6D-F), even exceeds that of the cells treated only with the y-CDs (Fig. 5C), suggesting that the combination of y-CDs and Fe3+ ions can also be applied for monitoring PPi in cells. Although the detection limit is much lower than that in aqueous solution, this scenario means it has a broad prospect for the future application of monitoring ATP hydrolysis and other physiological processes by monitoring intracellular PPi.

In summary, we have successfully prepared the y-CDs with a bright yellow fluorescence by a modified one-step hydrothermal method using OPD as a precursor [41,46]. The particle size of the as-obtained y-CDs is small and uniform with an average size of 2.6 nm. The fluorescence of the y-CDs could be specifically quenched by Fe3+, and then selectively recovered upon addition of PPi because of the stronger interaction between Fe3+ and PPi. The fluorescence brightness change of the y-CDs could be distinctly observed by the naked eyes. Based on these phenomena, a novel sensing system was established for “turn-off” detection of Fe3+ and “turn-on” determination of PPi with LODs of 22.1 and 73.9 nM, respectively. Compared with those of reported methods (Table S1), the presented method exhibits better sensitivity for assaying Fe3+ ions and PPi. Moreover, it was found that the fluorescent y-CDs exhibit a good biocompatibility and a non-toxic behavior toward A-549 cells. Strikingly, the y-CDs can be further successfully applied to intracellular monitoring and imaging of Fe3+ and PPi, indicative of their potential applications in bioscience. Our work provides a new idea for basic biological research, biological imaging and biological analysis, and extends the practical applications of CDs into the fields of analysis and biotechnology.
Jinyan Du: . Ying Yang: . Taili Shao: Visualization. Shuangqing Qi: . Ping Zhang: . Shujuan Zhuo: . Changqing Zhu: .

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.11.108.

ce:section.ce:para:
